metadata.title: Build a Fourier Bandwidth-Duration Uncertainty Demo with Claude Code + Manim
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = time-domain Gaussian; CRIMSON = frequency-domain FT. INK for all text.
metadata.note: Medhavy register. B00=brand intro; B01-B04=Fourier uncertainty; B05=brand outro.

beats.narration_text: Medhavy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

lines: — the perfect name for our AI-powered

beats.narration_text: The prompt. Claude Code terminal: claude — write a Manim scene, Fourier uncertainty. Gaussian pulse in time with sigma-t equals one picosecond. Show the pulse and its Fourier transform side by side. Label sigma-omega equals one over two-sigma-t equals five times ten-to-the-eleven radians per second. Three things: the rule, the numbers, what to render.

props.command:
claude "write a Manim scene:
  Gaussian pulse: f(t)=e^(−t²/2σ²), σ_t=1ps
  show FT: F(ω)∝e^(−ω²σ_t²/2)
  label σ_ω=5×10¹¹ rad/s
  two panels: time domain / frequency domain"

beats.narration_text: The CLI writes the file. Before running — read it. Find this line: sigma-omega equals one over two times sigma-t. That is the entire time-frequency uncertainty. Check: sigma-t equals one picosecond gives sigma-omega equals five times ten-to-the-eleven radians per second. That is about eighty gigahertz of bandwidth. The product sigma-t times sigma-omega equals exactly zero-point-five — the minimum. Thirty seconds.

props.code:
# fourier_unc.py  —  Claude Code output
import numpy as np

sigma_t = 1e-12   # s  (1 picosecond)

# ← the physics: time-frequency uncertainty
sigma_w = 1 / (2 * sigma_t)  # 5e11 rad/s

t = np.linspace(-5*sigma_t, 5*sigma_t, 300)
f_t = np.exp(-t**2 / (2*sigma_t**2))

omega = np.linspace(-3*sigma_w, 3*sigma_w, 300)
F_w = np.exp(-omega**2 * sigma_t**2 / 2)

# product: sigma_t * sigma_w = 0.500 (minimum)

beats.narration_text: Run it. Two things to verify. First: the product. Sigma-t times sigma-omega equals zero-point-five — exactly the minimum uncertainty. The Gaussian is the unique pulse that achieves the lower bound. Second: the shapes. Both pulse and its transform are Gaussian — a Gaussian in time gives a Gaussian in frequency. No other shape has a smaller product. Both checks pass.

Iterate: claude — update fourier-unc dot py — halve sigma-t to zero-point-five picoseconds. Watch sigma-omega double to ten-to-the-twelve. The time panel narrows; the frequency panel widens. One parameter. The product stays at zero-point-five. The loop closes.

props.command:
claude "update fourier_unc.py:
  → halve σ_t: 1 ps → 0.5 ps
  → show σ_ω doubles to 1e12 rad/s
  → product σ_t·σ_ω stays at 0.5"

beats.narration_text: Medhavy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at Medhavy A-I. Medhavy dot com.
